title: DFS
description: Preorder/Inorder/Postorder, generalized beyond trees: DFS on any graph-like structure, and a direct side-by-side against BFS.
objectives: Understand DFS as Preorder/Inorder/Postorder's general form
Implement recursive DFS on a grid with a visited set
Compare BFS and DFS's exploration order directly
Know when to reach for BFS vs DFS

sections.text: 1. Recap: The Depth-First Orders Were All DFS on a Tree

Preorder, Inorder, and Postorder all committed to one path before backtracking — that's depth-first behavior. Depth-First Search (DFS) is the general technique behind all three, applicable to any graph-like structure, not just trees.
DFS explores as far as possible down one path before backtracking to try another, commonly implemented with recursion (or an explicit stack) plus a visited set to handle cycles.
3. Full Example: DFS on a Grid

sections.title: Why the order looks so different from BFS
points: From (0,0), DFS goes down to (1,0), then IMMEDIATELY commits fully to exploring from THERE, rather than checking (0,0)'s other neighbors first
It keeps diving — (1,0) → (2,0) → (2,1) → (1,1) → (0,1) → (0,2) → (1,2) → (2,2) — a long, deep chain before finally backtracking
Compare to BFS's grid trace: 0,0 1,0 0,1 2,0 1,1 0,2 2,1 1,2 2,2 — BFS spreads outward evenly; DFS commits to one long path first

sections.text: 4. BFS vs DFS, Side by Side

rows: Explores level by level, outward evenly
Commits to one path fully before backtracking
Naturally finds the SHORTEST path in an unweighted graph

sections.text: This shortest-path property is a genuinely important practical reason to reach for BFS specifically: because it explores in expanding layers, the very first time it reaches a target node is guaranteed to be via the shortest possible number of steps, in an unweighted graph.

sections.title: Exploring a maze by committing to one corridor vs sending out scouts in every direction
sections.text: DFS is like walking into a maze and following one corridor as far as it goes before ever backtracking to try a different turn — you might end up very deep, very fast, down one path. BFS is like sending out scouts who explore one step outward in every direction simultaneously, guaranteeing that whichever scout reaches the exit first did so via the shortest possible route.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the visited set on a graph or grid — exactly the same cycle danger as BFS applies to DFS.
Assuming DFS finds the shortest path — it doesn't guarantee this the way BFS does; DFS just guarantees every reachable node gets visited eventually.
Confusing which structure each uses — BFS needs an explicit queue; DFS is naturally recursive (using the call stack, recall Stack Memory), though an explicit stack works too.
Marking visited at the wrong point relative to recursion — in DFS, marking at the very start of the function (before exploring neighbors) is what prevents infinite recursion on a cycle.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How does DFS relate to preorder/inorder/postorder traversal?
questions.answer: Those three orders are DFS applied specifically to trees; DFS itself is the general technique — commit to one path fully before backtracking — applicable to any graph-like structure.

questions.question: What is the key structural difference between how BFS and DFS are typically implemented?
questions.answer: BFS uses a queue (FIFO) to process nodes in discovery order; DFS uses recursion or an explicit stack (LIFO) to commit fully to one path before backtracking.

questions.question: Why is BFS, not DFS, typically used to find the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
questions.answer: BFS explores in expanding layers outward from the start, so the first time it reaches any node is guaranteed to be via the fewest possible steps — DFS makes no such guarantee, since it can reach a node via a long, roundabout path first.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: DFS generalizes preorder, inorder, and postorder traversal beyond trees: commit fully to exploring one path before backtracking to try another, typically implemented via recursion (using the call stack) or an explicit stack, plus a visited set to handle cycles on graphs and grids. Compared side by side with BFS, DFS dives deep first while BFS spreads outward evenly level by level — which is exactly why BFS, not DFS, is used to find the shortest path in an unweighted graph: reaching a node for the first time via BFS's expanding layers guarantees that path used the fewest possible steps.

sections.title: Trick to Remember DFS
sections.text: DFS = walk one maze corridor to its end before backtracking. BFS = send scouts outward evenly, first to arrive = shortest path. DFS: recursion/stack. BFS: queue.

sections.title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth.
hints: A tree is just a graph with no cycles — DFS applies directly, diving down each branch fully before backtracking.
The recursive call stack IS the DFS 'commit to one path' behavior — no explicit visited set is needed on a tree, since there's exactly one path to every node.
Depth = 1 + the deeper of the two recursive DFS calls.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: BFS layer by layer, counting completed layers — the opposite exploration strategy from DFS.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: DFS: dive to the bottom of each branch, combine on the way back up.

sections.title: Path Sum
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree and targetSum, return true if a root-to-leaf path sums to targetSum.
hints: DFS commits to one root-to-leaf path at a time — exactly what's needed here, checking each complete path's sum as it's fully explored.
Carry a shrinking 'remaining' target down through the recursion instead of an accumulating sum.
A leaf succeeds only if the remaining target hits exactly 0 there.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Collect every root-to-leaf path's total sum, then check if targetSum is among them.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: DFS with a shrinking target, checking exactly at each leaf, no list of sums stored.

sections.title: Path Sum II
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree and targetSum, return ALL root-to-leaf paths that sum to targetSum.
hints: This is Path Sum's DFS-with-shrinking-target idea, but now the actual PATH needs to be tracked, not just whether a valid one exists — exactly Backtracking's choose/explore/unchoose pattern.
Push the current node's value onto a running path before recursing into children (CHOOSE), and pop it back off after both children have been explored (UNCHOOSE).
A leaf with remaining target 0 means the current running path is a valid answer — copy it into the results.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Collect every root-to-leaf path with its sum, then filter afterward for matches.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Backtracking with a shrinking target: choose (push), explore, unchoose (pop) — a valid path is recorded the instant it's found, no separate sum pass.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n²) worst case (copying paths), O(n) traversal
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) call stack, O(n) for results

sections.title: Binary Tree Paths
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return all root-to-leaf paths as strings, in any order.
hints: The same choose/explore/unchoose DFS pattern as Path Sum II — but building a STRING path instead of checking a sum.
Build the path string as recursion descends; at a leaf, the current path string is a complete answer.

No explicit 'unchoose' string-trimming is required if each recursive call constructs its OWN new string (path + "->" + value) rather than mutating a shared one — a simpler variant of backtracking without manual undo.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Collect all (node, ancestor list) pairs first, then build path strings for every leaf found.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n²) — rebuilding strings from stored node lists

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Build the path string directly during a single DFS pass, appending a complete result at each leaf.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n²) worst case for string building, O(n) traversal
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) call stack, O(n) for results

sections.title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
sections.problemStatement: Given a general (not necessarily BST) binary tree and two nodes p and q, return their lowest common ancestor.
hints: Without BST ordering, there's no shortcut telling you which side to search — DFS must explore BOTH subtrees.
Recursively search left and right for p and q. If a subtree's search returns a non-null result, that subtree contains at least one of them.
If BOTH the left and right searches return non-null, the CURRENT node is exactly where p and q's paths split — the answer. If only one side is non-null, pass that result upward.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Find the full root-to-node path for both p and q separately, then compare the paths to find where they last agree.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A single DFS pass: a node is the LCA the moment both its subtrees report finding one of p/q each.

sections.title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return the maximum path sum of any path (a sequence of connected nodes, not necessarily through the root, each node used at most once).
hints: This combines Diameter of Binary Tree's 'best value through this node' idea with a running global answer, but for SUMS instead of path lengths.
Recall Diameter's insight: at each node, the best path THROUGH it is left's contribution + right's contribution + the node itself — update a global best with this at every node.

A subtree's contribution to its PARENT (what gets returned upward) is different: only the node plus the BETTER single side, since a path continuing upward can't branch both ways — and negative contributions should be discarded (treated as 0) rather than dragging the sum down.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every node, compute the max downward path sum starting there in each direction separately, combining fresh at every node.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A single post-order pass computes each node's downward contribution AND updates the global best simultaneously.
